meta.title: Mercedes GLK X204 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: Mercedes GLK X204 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first-generation GLK compact SUV — from the four-cylinder OM651 diesel to the V6 M272 petrol and the torque-rich OM642 V6 diesel. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the MRA platform that defined Mercedes' compact off-roader. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes GLK X204 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X204 specifically — including the pre- and post-facelift changes that introduced the M276 and M274 families.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: * OM651.912/916: 95% confidence — inherited from the Model Page's verified figures for the wider Mercedes range. * M272.948/991: 85% confidence — well‑documented M272 V6; pattern data. * M276.957: 85% confidence — well‑regarded M276; pattern data. * M274.920: 85% confidence — well‑documented M274; pattern data. * OM642.832/961: 80% confidence — V6 diesel with known issues; provisional.

overview.intro: The X204 is the first generation of the Mercedes‑Benz GLK‑Class, a compact luxury crossover SUV unveiled at the 2008 Beijing Auto Show and launched later that year. Built on a modified version of the W204 C‑Class platform, it offered a boxy, rugged design language that set it apart from rivals like the BMW X3 and Audi Q5. The engine range spanned from the 2.1‑litre OM651 four‑cylinder diesel to the 3.5‑litre M272 and M276 V6 petrol units, and the 3.0‑litre OM642 V6 diesel. A significant facelift arrived in 2012, bringing new engines including the M276 V6 petrol and updated OM651 diesel variants. Production ended in 2015, succeeded by the GLC‑Class (X253).
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W204-based (shared with C‑Class W204) • Predecessor: none (new model line) • Successor: GLC‑Class (X253, 2015–present) • Sibling body styles in this generation: none (GLK is standalone compact SUV)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The X204 GLK generated 1,250+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM651 diesel as the most‑requested powertrain.

items.quote: Mercedes' own 2.1‑litre four‑cylinder diesel, introduced in 2008, is the pick of the X204 diesel range — efficient, refined, and backed by a strong reliability record when serviced properly. The 250 CDI variant delivers 204 bhp with 500 Nm of torque.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern, efficient diesel for high‑mileage SUV motoring.

items.quote: Mercedes' 3.5‑litre V6, introduced with the 2012 facelift, replaced the M272 with a more refined and reliable unit. It delivers 306 bhp with a silky character and strong torque.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 petrol refinement without the pre‑facelift M272's balance shaft concerns.

items.quote: The M272 V6 petrol is known for balance shaft sprocket wear on pre‑2010 models — a manufacturing defect that can lead to timing chain issues and catastrophic engine damage if ignored. Post‑2010 models are significantly improved.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 petrol power but must verify the balance shaft has been addressed.

items.engine: OM642.832/961 (GLK 320 CDI / 350 CDI) — oil cooler seal leak
items.quote: The V6 diesel's oil cooler seals are prone to failure, leaking oil into the V of the engine. Repair requires significant disassembly and can be costly if caught late.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 diesel torque but can afford the premium maintenance.

items.quote: The mid‑range diesel offers the best balance of performance (170 bhp), economy (up to 44 mpg), and reliability. It's the sweet spot in the X204 range.

The 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder turbo petrol delivers 211 bhp with genuine performance and strong torque. It's often overlooked in favour of the diesels but offers a compelling blend of power and efficiency.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol power without the V6 running costs.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Balance shaft wear (pre‑2010), intake manifold flaps
rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — pre‑2010 cars need balance shaft history.
Good Value — post‑2010 M272 is improved.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Injector seal leaks (Black Death), DPF clogging
Oil cooler seal leak, swirl flap motor
rows.ownershipVerdict: V6 Diesel — smooth but watch for oil leaks.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Oil cooler seal leak, AdBlue/SCR issues
rows.ownershipVerdict: Powerful Diesel — more power, more maintenance.

economicsRule.title: The X204 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800–£4,800 OM651 recon against a £4,000–£12,000 X204 sits at 23–120% of the car's value. On a sub‑£5,000 X204, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 35–60% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X204 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: GLK 220 CDI (OM651) — best balance of economy and reliability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X204 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: GLK 350 CDI (OM642) — V6 diesel running costs add up.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X204 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: GLK 220 CDI (OM651) — the sweet spot in the range.

problems.title: OM651 Injector Seal Leaks (Black Death)
problems.rootCause: The injector seals on the OM651 can fail, allowing combustion gases to escape past the injector. This creates a carbon buildup that sounds like a ticking noise and can eventually damage the cylinder head.
problems.recommendation: If you hear a ticking noise from the engine bay, have the injector seals checked immediately — a £400–£1,000 repair is far cheaper than the £2,800–£4,800 replacement that follows if the head is damaged.

cta.label: Compare OM651 engine prices for your X204 diesel →

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Wear (pre‑2010)
problems.rootCause: The M272's balance shaft sprocket can wear prematurely due to a manufacturing defect, leading to timing chain issues and, if ignored, catastrophic engine failure.
problems.recommendation: On pre‑2010 GLK 300 and 350 models, have the balance shaft inspected at 60,000 miles — an £800–£1,800 preventative repair is cheap insurance against a £2,500–£5,500 engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare M272 engine prices for your X204 V6 petrol →

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak
problems.rootCause: The OM642's oil cooler seals (located in the V of the engine) can degrade and leak, allowing oil to mix with coolant or leak onto the engine. Repair requires significant disassembly.
problems.recommendation: If you notice oil in the coolant or a burning oil smell on a GLK 320 CDI or 350 CDI, have the oil cooler seals inspected immediately — an £800–£1,500 repair is far cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows if the engine overheats.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your X204 V6 diesel →

problems.title: M276 Coolant Leaks (facelift V6)
problems.rootCause: The M276's water pump and plastic coolant fittings can degrade over time, leading to coolant loss and potential overheating.
problems.recommendation: Replace the water pump proactively at 60,000 miles — a £400–£800 repair is cheap insurance against a £2,500–£4,500 engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare M276 engine prices for your X204 V6 petrol →

replacementCosts.h2: X204 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — the X204 shares its platform with the C‑Class (W204), so engine‑bay access and labour times are consistent across the range. Costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are X204 first‑generation GLK only. The GLK was replaced by the GLC‑Class (X253) in 2015.
